title: practical english usage - Part 352
content:
Since can be used as a conjunction of time, introducing its own clause.

3 s/nce-clause:      since      we    were      at   school It had been ages since that wonderful trip.

It's been a long time since the last meeting.

American English prefers perfect tenses in this structure.

vocabulary’  •  582  so: adverb meaning ‘ like this/that’ For do so, ►    °9.

For so with say and tell, ►     586.   For so am I, etc, ►    309.       For so with Hope, believe, etc, ►     585.

lie says he is ill and he looks it. (     .. .-he not I don’t think we should do it in that way. (     .. . de it se.) not When he laughs like that I want to scream, (     When he laughs so. ..) not Look - hold it up in the air like this, (     .. . held it up in the air so;) ‘ in this/that way'.

In other cases, so is not normally used adverbially to mean 'like this/that’ , 2  not   used    in  other     cases I read the front page very carefully, and the rest of the paper less so.

She was always nervous, and after her accident she became even more so.

The weather is stormy, and will remain so over the weekend.

‘ Will the business make a loss this year? ‘ It appears so.’ that way'. This happens, for example, after seem, appear, remain, more and less.

So can be used in a formal style in a few cases to mean 'like this/that’ , 'in this/ 1  after    seem,      appear,        etc 582  so:  adverb     meaning       'like   this/that' ‘ What are you doing? Tm  smelling the meat to see if it’s still OK. ’ something. Progressive forms can be used.

Another transitive use is to say that we  are using our noses to find out 'investigate' 4 transitive       verb: I can smell supper.

As we walked into the house, we smelt something burning.

Progressive forms are not used. We   often use can smell (►  84).

Smell can be used with an object, to say what we  perceive with our noses.

3 transitive       verb:   'perceive' That dog smells.

Smell is sometimes used to mean 'smell bad'.

His aftershave smelt like an explosion in a flower shop.

The railway carriage smelt of beer and old socks.

Before a noun, smell of and smell like are used.

The soup smells funny. What’s in it? (     . . .-is smellingftmny-...) not Those roses smell beautiful, (     .. .smeU-beautffidfy?) to say how  something smells. Progressive forms are not used.

Smell can be used as a linking verb (►   1 1), followed by an adjective or noun, 2  linking     verb common: smelled and smelt. American forms are usually regular.

In British English, regular and irregular past tenses and participles are both 1  British    and   American      forms 581  sme//  _________         ___ Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary •  583  so (conjunction) and then Why are you driving so fast?

The milk was so good that we couldn 't stop drinking it.

We   can use so before an adjective alone (without a noun) or an adverb.

5 before     adjectives        and   adverbs fast that.. .) not He spoke so fast that nobody could understand, (     Hesp&k-e very we stopped playing.) not It was so cold that we stopped playing, (     It was very cold that We   use so, not very, before r/zar-clauses.

He's so bad-tempered! (= How  bad-tempered he is!)   You're so right!

an exclamation (►  223).

the speaker wishes to emphasise what is said. This structure is rather like In an informal style, so can also be used like very to give new information, when 3 emphatic       use known) I didn't think it would be so warm, (referring to information which  is already - It was very warm in Scotland, (giving new information) I'm sorry I'm so late, (referring to information which  is already known) - You're very late, (giving new information) or which  is obvious. Compare: refer to information which  has already been given, which  is already known, Very (►  616) is used when we  are giving new information. So is mainly used to 2  so   and   very I wish she didn't drive so fast.

because of that we  couldn't go out.) It was so cold that we couldn't go out. (= It was very cold weather, and I'm sorry you're so tired. (= I know you’ re very tired, and I'm sorry.) high degree of some quality - in situations where very is also a suitable word.
